request.question: How old was the man for whom Āryabhaṭa numeration is named when he wrote Āryabhaṭīya?

title: Aryabhata
Sakan Ryokan
Can I Play with Madness
Āryabhaṭa numeration
The Water Man
Edward Page Mitchell
Jean Claude Jacob
Antidosis
Morishima Chūryō
Simon Sheppard (writer)
sentences: Aryabhata (IAST: Āryabhaṭa ) or Aryabhata I (476–550 CE) was the first of the major mathematician-astronomers from the classical age of Indian mathematics and Indian astronomy.
His works include the "Āryabhaṭīya" (499 CE, when he was 23 years old) and the "Arya-siddhanta".
Sakan Ryokan or "Hotel Sakan" (ホテル佐勘) is an historic ryokan (Japanese inn) located in Sendai, Miyagi Prefecture, Japan.
The inn is over 1,000 years old, and is located within the spa resort named "Akiu Spa," which has four communal onsen (baths) that are supplied by natural hot springs.
The hot springs are one of the three oldest ones in Japan, and are located near the Natori River.
The inn was originally started by a man named Kanzaburo Satoh, whom the inn is named after.
Ownership of the inn has been handed down to Satoh's descendants for 34 generations.
It is located next to the 400 year old Iwanumaya Hotel.
"Can I Play with Madness" is a song by the English heavy metal band Iron Maiden.
The song is the sixteenth single released by the band.
Released in 1988, it was the first single from their seventh studio album, "Seventh Son of a Seventh Son" (1988), and hit number 3 in the UK charts.
The song is about a young man who wants to learn the future from an old prophet with a crystal ball.
The young man thinks he is going mad and seeks the old prophet to help him cope with his visions/nightmares.
The prophet's advice is ignored by the young man and they become angry with each other.
The song was originally a ballad named "On the Wings of Eagles", written by Adrian Smith.
The Āryabhaṭa numeration is a system of numerals based on Sanskrit phonemes.
It was introduced in the early 6th century in India by Āryabhaṭa, in the first chapter titled "Gītika Padam" of his "Aryabhatiya".
It attributes a numerical value to each syllable of the form consonant+vowel possible in Sanskrit phonology, from "ka " = 1 up to "hau " = 10.
"The Water Man" (Slovene: "Povodni mož" ) was the first Slovene ballad.

It was written in the spring of 1826 by the Slovene Romantic poet France Prešeren and was a narration about Urška, a flirt from Ljubljana that ended in the hands of a handsome man who happened to be a vodyanoy ("povodni mož" ).
The poem was based on a story from "The Glory of Carniola", about a dance at Old Square in Ljubljana in July 1547, when Urška Šefer was enchanted by a vodyanoy and tugged to the Ljubljanica.
Prešeren wrote it due to his unfulfilled love towards Zalika Dolenc.
In the first publication of the poem Urška was named Zalika.
Later, in Prešeren's Poems (1847), she was named Urška.
Edward Page Mitchell (1852–1927) was an American editorial and short story writer for "The Sun", a daily newspaper in New York City.
He became that newspaper's editor in 1897, succeeding Charles Anderson Dana.
Mitchell was recognized as a major figure in the early development of the science fiction genre.

Mitchell wrote fiction about a man rendered invisible by scientific means ("The Crystal Man", published in 1881) before H.G. Wells's "The Invisible Man", wrote about a time-travel machine ("The Clock that Went Backward") before Wells's "The Time Machine", wrote about faster-than-light travel ("The Tachypomp"; now perhaps his best-known work) in 1874, a thinking computer and a cyborg in 1879 ("The Ablest Man in the World"), and also wrote the earliest known stories about matter transmission or teleportation ("The Man without a Body", 1877) and a superior mutant ("Old Squids and Little Speller").
"Exchanging Their Souls" (1877) is one of the earliest fictional accounts of mind transfer.
Mitchell retired in 1926, a year before dying of a cerebral hemorrhage.

Jean Claude Jacob was a serf from the Jura Mountains, supposedly 120 years old, who was brought from his native place to figure as "dean of the human race" in Paris at the Festival of the Federation of July 1790.
Thomas Carlyle wrote of Jacob in :Having seen the Human Species itself, to have seen the 'Dean of the Human Species,' ceased now to be a miracle.

Such 'Doyen du Genre Humain, Eldest of Men,' had shewn himself there, in these weeks: Jean Claude Jacob, a born Serf, deputed from his native Jura Mountains to thank the National Assembly for enfranchising them.
On his bleached worn face are ploughed the furrowings of one hundred and twenty years.

He has heard dim patois-talk, of immortal Grand-Monarch victories; of a burnt Palatinate, as he toiled and moiled to make a little speck of this Earth greener; of Cevennes Dragoonings; of Marlborough going to the war.
Four generations have bloomed out, and loved and hated, and rustled off: he was forty-six when Louis Fourteenth died.
The Assembly, as one man, spontaneously rose, and did reverence to the Eldest of the World; old Jean is to take seance among them, honourably, with covered head.
He gazes feebly there, with his old eyes, on that new wonder-scene; dreamlike to him, and uncertain, wavering amid fragments of old memories and dreams.
For Time is all growing unsubstantial, dreamlike; Jean's eyes and mind are weary, and about to close, — and open on a far other wonder-scene, which shall be real.
Patriot Subscription, Royal Pension was got for him, and he returned home glad; but in two months more he left it all, and went on his unknown way.
Antidosis (Ancient Greek ἀντίδοσις ), is the title of a speech treatise by the ancient Greek rhetorician, Isocrates.
The "Antidosis" can be viewed as a defense, an autobiography, or rhetorical treatise.
However, since Isocrates wrote it when he was 82 years old, it is generally seen by some people as an autobiography.
The title literally translates as “an exchange,” and was applied in ancient Greek courts as a peculiar law pertaining to an exchange of estates between two parties.

If one of the 1200 wealthiest Athenians eligible was tasked with the performance of a public liturgy and financing one of the many public concerns of Athens, then he could avoid the duty by nominating a richer man who was more qualified than himself to perform it.
If the supposedly richer man disagreed with the terms, then the entirety of their estates would be exchanged and the now more wealthy man would have to perform the liturgy, as originally planned.

The law inspired Isocrates' "Antidosis", which was written in the form of a court case where Isocrates had to defend himself from a charge of corrupting the youth by teaching them how to speak well in order for them to gain an unfair advantage over their peers.
Although this work is put forward by Isocrates as his imagined defense in a legal case, it is more a treatise on morality and teaching.
Morishima Chūryō (森島 中良 , 1756 – December 29, 1810) was an Edo period Japanese author of popular fiction who also wrote a number of works in the field of rangaku (Western studies).
He wrote under many pen names, including Manzōtei, Shinra Manzō (or, conventionally, Shinra Banshō), and Tenjiku Rōjin ("old man from India").
The latter constituted an allusion to the pen name Tenjiku Rōnin ("masterless samurai from India"), used by Hiraga Gennai, to whom Chūryō was the principal literary successor.
Chūryō co-authored several plays with Gennai early in his career, and went on to write in almost all of the many genres of popular fiction that were collectively known as gesaku.
He also wrote kyōka, or comic waka poetry, under the pen name Taketsue no Sugaru.
Chūryō was the younger brother of Katsuragawa Hoshū, a shogunal physician and leading scholar of rangaku.
Simon Sheppard is a writer of gay erotica and a sex-advice columnist from San Francisco.
He is the author of many books of gay sex writing, including "Man on Man: The Best of Simon Sheppard", "Sodomy!"
, "Jockboys," "Kinkorama: Dispatches From the Front Lines of Perversion," "In Deep", and "Sex Parties 101".

He is also the editor of "Homosex: 60 Years of Gay Erotica," winner of the 2007 Lambda Literary Award for LGBT erotica; the anthology "Leathermen"; and is the coeditor of the anthologies "Rough Stuff" and "Roughed Up."
Sheppard's work is wide-ranging, often combining history, philosophy, and culture — high and low — with hardcore sex.

His first book, "Hotter Than Hell and Other Stories", won the Erotic Authors Association Award for Best Collection of the Year, and the title story of "In Deep" was shortlisted for the Rauxa Prize for Erotic Fiction.
His work has also appeared in over 300 anthologies and magazines, including many editions of "Best Gay Erotica" and "The Best American Erotica".
He wrote the syndicated column "Sex Talk," and the online columns "Perv" and "Notes of a Cranky Old Fag."
The online serial "The Dirty Boys' Club,” which he wrote for OutPersonals, was published as a novel in 2012.
He also curates and co-hosts, with Carol Queen, the San Francisco performance series Perverts Put Out!
Sheppard is openly gay, and is active in the queer artistic, political and AIDS-activist communities.
He lives with his husband in San Francisco, where "San Francisco" magazine dubbed him “our erotica king.”

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: How old was the man for whom Āryabhaṭa numeration is named when he wrote Āryabhaṭīya?
hop1_docs: Aryabhata | Aryabhata (IAST: Āryabhaṭa ) or Aryabhata I (476–550 CE) was the first of the major mathematician-astronomers from the classical age of Indian mathematics and Indian astronomy.  His works include the "Āryabhaṭīya" (499 CE, when he was 23 years old) and the "Arya-siddhanta".

Āryabhaṭa's sine table | Āryabhaṭa's sine table is a set of twenty-four numbers given in the astronomical treatise Āryabhaṭīya composed by the fifth century Indian mathematician and astronomer Āryabhaṭa (476–550 CE), for the computation of the half-chords of certain set of arcs of a circle.  It is not a table in the modern sense of a mathematical table; that is, it is not a set of numbers arranged into rows and columns.

Āryabhaṭa numeration | The Āryabhaṭa numeration is a system of numerals based on Sanskrit phonemes.  It was introduced in the early 6th century in India by Āryabhaṭa, in the first chapter titled "Gītika Padam" of his "Aryabhatiya".  It attributes a numerical value to each syllable of the form consonant+vowel possible in Sanskrit phonology, from "ka " = 1 up to "hau " = 10.

How You Remind Me | "How You Remind Me" is a song by Canadian rock band Nickelback.  It was released on August 21, 2001 as the lead single from their third studio album "Silver Side Up" (2001).  A "Gold Mix" was made for latter editions of the single with the heavier guitars edited out of the chorus.  Lead vocalist and guitarist Chad Kroeger wrote the song about his old girlfriend Jodi, with whom he had a dysfunctional relationship.  He referred to this song as the song that put Nickelback on the map when played at their concert in Sturgis, South Dakota, and is often considered to be their signature song.

Aryabhata II | Āryabhaṭa (c. 920 – c. 1000) was an ArIndian mathematician and astronomer, and the author of the "Maha-Siddhanta".  The numeral II is given to him to distinguish him from the earlier and more influential Āryabhaṭa I.

Kuṭṭaka | Kuṭṭaka is an algorithm for finding integer solutions of linear Diophantine equations.  A linear Diophantine equation is an equation of the form "ax" + "by" = "c" where "x" and "y" are unknown quantities and "a", "b" and "c" are known quantities with integer values.  The algorithm was originally invented by the Indian astronomer-mathematician Āryabhaṭa (476–550 CE) and is described very briefly in his Āryabhaṭīya.  Āryabhaṭa did not give the algorithm the name "Kuṭṭaka" and his description of the method was mostly obscure and incomprehensible.  It was Bhaskara I (c. 600 – c. 680), who gave a detailed description of the algorithm with several examples from astronomy in his "Āryabhatiyabhāṣya", who gave the algorithm the name "Kuṭṭaka".  In Sanskrit, the word Kuṭṭaka means "pulverization" (reducing to powder) and it indicates the nature of the algorithm.  The algorithm in essence is a process where the coefficients in a given linear Diophantine equation are broken up into smaller numbers to get a linear Diophantine equation with smaller coefficients.  In general, it is easy to find integer solutions of linear Diophantine equations with small coefficients.  From a solution to the reduced equation a solution to the original equation can be determined.  Many Indian mathematicians after Aryabhaṭa have discussed the Kuṭṭaka method with variations and refinements.  The Kuṭṭaka method was considered to be so important that the entire subject of algebra used to be called "Kuṭṭaka-ganita" or simply "Kuṭṭaka".  Sometimes the subject of solving linear Diophantine equations is also called "Kuṭṭaka".

The Naming of Cats | "The Naming of Cats" is a poem in T. S. Eliot's poetry book "Old Possum's Book of Practical Cats" and its stage adaptation, Andrew Lloyd Webber's popular musical "Cats".  It describes to humans how cats get their names.  The poem has also been quoted in other films, notably "Logan's Run", when Logan meets the old man outside the dome city.
response.summary_1:
Primary topic: Āryabhaṭa (author of Āryabhaṭīya) and the Āryabhaṭa numeration

- Fact: "Aryabhata I (476–550 CE)" (passage identifies Āryabhaṭa / Aryabhata I).
- Fact: Āryabhaṭa's work "Āryabhaṭīya" dated "499 CE, when he was 23 years old" (verbatim from passage).
- Fact: "The Āryabhaṭa numeration" was "introduced in the early 6th century" by Āryabhaṭa in the first chapter titled "Gītika Padam" of his "Aryabhatiya" (verbatim fragments).
- Ambiguity/missing info: the name "Āryabhaṭa" is ambiguous — candidate persons include "Āryabhaṭa I (476–550 CE)" and "Āryabhaṭa II (c. 920 – c. 1000)"; passages attribute the numeration to "Āryabhaṭa" without always using the numeric suffix, though passage [1] ties Āryabhaṭīya to Aryabhata I.
- Next-hop retrieval directives (priority):
  1. Fetch "Āryabhaṭa" (Aryabhata I) Wikipedia page — inspect lede/infobox and the "Works" or "Āryabhaṭīya" section to confirm composition date and age statement.
  2. Fetch "Āryabhaṭa numeration" page — inspect lede and the "Gītika Padam" / first-chapter details to confirm attribution and wording.

hop2_docs: Aryabhata | Aryabhata (IAST: Āryabhaṭa ) or Aryabhata I (476–550 CE) was the first of the major mathematician-astronomers from the classical age of Indian mathematics and Indian astronomy.  His works include the "Āryabhaṭīya" (499 CE, when he was 23 years old) and the "Arya-siddhanta".

Āryabhaṭa's sine table | Āryabhaṭa's sine table is a set of twenty-four numbers given in the astronomical treatise Āryabhaṭīya composed by the fifth century Indian mathematician and astronomer Āryabhaṭa (476–550 CE), for the computation of the half-chords of certain set of arcs of a circle.  It is not a table in the modern sense of a mathematical table; that is, it is not a set of numbers arranged into rows and columns.

Kuṭṭaka | Kuṭṭaka is an algorithm for finding integer solutions of linear Diophantine equations.  A linear Diophantine equation is an equation of the form "ax" + "by" = "c" where "x" and "y" are unknown quantities and "a", "b" and "c" are known quantities with integer values.  The algorithm was originally invented by the Indian astronomer-mathematician Āryabhaṭa (476–550 CE) and is described very briefly in his Āryabhaṭīya.  Āryabhaṭa did not give the algorithm the name "Kuṭṭaka" and his description of the method was mostly obscure and incomprehensible.  It was Bhaskara I (c. 600 – c. 680), who gave a detailed description of the algorithm with several examples from astronomy in his "Āryabhatiyabhāṣya", who gave the algorithm the name "Kuṭṭaka".  In Sanskrit, the word Kuṭṭaka means "pulverization" (reducing to powder) and it indicates the nature of the algorithm.  The algorithm in essence is a process where the coefficients in a given linear Diophantine equation are broken up into smaller numbers to get a linear Diophantine equation with smaller coefficients.  In general, it is easy to find integer solutions of linear Diophantine equations with small coefficients.  From a solution to the reduced equation a solution to the original equation can be determined.  Many Indian mathematicians after Aryabhaṭa have discussed the Kuṭṭaka method with variations and refinements.  The Kuṭṭaka method was considered to be so important that the entire subject of algebra used to be called "Kuṭṭaka-ganita" or simply "Kuṭṭaka".  Sometimes the subject of solving linear Diophantine equations is also called "Kuṭṭaka".

Āryabhaṭa numeration | The Āryabhaṭa numeration is a system of numerals based on Sanskrit phonemes.  It was introduced in the early 6th century in India by Āryabhaṭa, in the first chapter titled "Gītika Padam" of his "Aryabhatiya".  It attributes a numerical value to each syllable of the form consonant+vowel possible in Sanskrit phonology, from "ka " = 1 up to "hau " = 10.

Aryabhata II | Āryabhaṭa (c. 920 – c. 1000) was an ArIndian mathematician and astronomer, and the author of the "Maha-Siddhanta".  The numeral II is given to him to distinguish him from the earlier and more influential Āryabhaṭa I.

Huan Tan | Huan Tan 桓譚 (c. 43 BCE–28 CE) was a Chinese philosopher of the Han Dynasty (202 BCE–220 CE) and short-lived interregnum of the Xin Dynasty (9–23 CE).  Huan's mode of philosophical thought belonged to an Old Text realist tradition supported by other contemporaries such as the naturalist and mechanistic philosopher Wang Chong (27–c. 100), the latter who Rafe de Crespigny states was probably heavily influenced by Huan Tan.  Huan was a close associate of the court astronomer and mathematician Liu Xin (c. 50 BCE–23 CE) as well as the author and poet Yang Xiong (53 BCE–18 CE), and worked as an official under the administrations of Emperor Ai of Han (r. 27–1 BCE), Wang Mang (r. 9–23 CE), Emperor Gengshi of Han (r. 23–25 CE), and Emperor Guangwu of Han (r. 25–57 CE).  He drew explicitly on Legalism in his writings on government, saying that in certain historical epochs harsher punishments are needed.

Paramartha | Paramārtha (Sanskrit: परमार्थ "Paramārtha"; : "Zhēndì" ) (499-569 CE) was an Indian monk from Ujjain in central India, who is best known for his prolific Chinese translations which include Vasubandhu's "Abhidharmakośa".  Paramārtha is considered one of the greatest translators of sutras in Chinese Buddhism, along with Kumārajīva and Xuanzang.
response.summary_2: Āryabhaṭa's work Āryabhaṭīya was composed in 499 CE, when he was 23 years old. Āryabhaṭa (476–550 CE) was an Indian mathematician-astronomer who introduced the Āryabhaṭa numeration in the first chapter "Gītika Padam" of his Āryabhaṭīya.
